stories.headline: Trump declares Iran war 'nearing completion' in prime-time address but vows to hit harder; oil surges past $105
stories.body: President Trump used a prime-time address from the White House on Wednesday evening to declare the month-long war in Iran a success nearing completion, but vowed to strike the country extremely hard over the next two to three weeks. In the 19-minute speech, Trump said the US had decimated Iran's navy, drone and ballistic missile forces and that the country was no longer a threat, though he offered no timeline for ending the conflict and threatened to destroy Iran's power grid and oil infrastructure if negotiations fail. Oil markets were unimpressed, with Brent crude jumping nearly 5 percent to $106 a barrel after the address, as traders saw little sign the closure of the Strait of Hormuz would ease anytime soon. The speech came hours after Iran fired a fresh barrage of missiles at Israel and Israel carried out two waves of attacks on Tehran, claiming to have killed a senior Hezbollah commander. Meanwhile, British Prime Minister Keir Starmer announced the UK would host 35 countries for talks on reopening the strait, and said he would seek a deeper partnership with the EU on defence, trade and energy, calling it a partnership for a dangerous world.

stories.headline: Artemis II launches successfully, sending four astronauts around the Moon for the first time in over 50 years
stories.body: NASA's Artemis II mission blasted off from Kennedy Space Center early Thursday, sending four astronauts on a 10-day journey around the Moon, the first crewed lunar voyage since Apollo 17 in 1972. The 32-storey Space Launch System rocket carried commander Reid Wiseman, pilot Victor Glover, mission specialist Christina Koch and Canadian astronaut Jeremy Hansen into orbit after loading more than 700,000 gallons of hydrogen and oxygen fuel. Glover becomes the first Black astronaut to travel to the Moon and Koch the first woman on a lunar trajectory. The crew will fly past the Moon's far side, reaching a record distance of some 4,000 miles beyond the lunar surface before returning for a Pacific splashdown. Tensions had been high after dangerous hydrogen leaks during earlier countdown tests forced lengthy delays, but the Orion spacecraft entered orbit successfully and is working to deploy its full systems before heading toward the Moon.

stories.headline: Magnitude 7.4 earthquake strikes off Indonesia, killing at least one and sparking tsunami warnings
stories.body: A powerful 7.4-magnitude earthquake struck in the Molucca Sea off Indonesia's Ternate island early Thursday at a depth of 35 kilometres, killing at least one person and triggering tsunami warnings across the region. A 70-year-old woman in North Sulawesi died after being crushed by building debris, and at least one other person was injured jumping from a building. The quake shook furniture and cracked roads across Manado and Bitung on the island of Sulawesi, with residents describing it as one of the strongest they had felt in at least six years. Schools evacuated chaotically as parents rushed to collect their children, and several stores lost power. The Pacific Tsunami Warning Center initially warned of possible waves along the coasts of Guam, Japan, Malaysia, Papua New Guinea, the Philippines and Taiwan, though the alert was lifted after two hours. At least two significant aftershocks followed.

stories.headline: Russia blocks Telegram for most users in latest internet censorship escalation
stories.body: Russia has blocked Telegram for more than 70 percent of its users, cutting off access to the country's dominant messaging app used by over 100 million people. The move has caused the biggest backlash of any internet restriction to date in Russia, with 55 percent of citizens opposing it according to a poll by the independent Levada Centre. Russians use Telegram not only for personal messaging but as their primary news source through channels run by official and opposition media, commentators and even the Kremlin itself. The military has also raised alarm, with soldiers warning that Telegram is their main communication tool on the front lines of the war in Ukraine. Authorities refused all protest permits, citing pandemic-era restrictions, and detained nearly 20 organisers who merely filed applications. Opposition parties in parliament broke with their usual deference to challenge the decision, with one deputy comparing the censorship to Soviet-era book banning. The official reason for the block is that Telegram's founder Pavel Durov has refused to cooperate with Russian intelligence services.

stories.headline: Swedish PM offers to bring far-right Sweden Democrats into government for first time
stories.body: Swedish Prime Minister Ulf Kristersson announced that the far-right Sweden Democrats would hold important ministerial posts in immigration and integration if his four-party coalition wins September's general election, marking a watershed moment in Swedish politics. The party, which has neo-Nazi roots and became Sweden's second largest after the 2022 election, currently serves only as a supporting partner to Kristersson's minority government. Standing alongside SD leader Jimmie Akesson, Kristersson said they had agreed to form a strong majority government if voters give them a mandate. The Left party's leader called the prospect disgusting and urged others to offer a different path for Sweden. SD's policies include stopping people from countries outside Sweden's immediate area from claiming asylum, a measure that would contravene international human rights law, and Akesson has publicly claimed that being Muslim and being Swedish is a contradiction.

stories.headline: SpaceX files for IPO that could make Elon Musk the world's first trillionaire
stories.body: SpaceX has made a confidential filing with the US Securities and Exchange Commission for an initial public offering that would make it one of the most valuable publicly traded companies in the world, with an expected valuation surpassing one trillion dollars. The IPO, which could raise 50 billion dollars or more, is aimed for June and would put Musk on track to become the world's first trillionaire based on his holdings in the company. SpaceX absorbed Musk's artificial intelligence venture xAI earlier this year, which itself had previously taken over the social media platform X, and the combined entity is believed to be the most valuable private company in the world at an internal valuation of 1.25 trillion dollars. The company, which Musk founded in 2002 to reduce the cost of space launches, would also partner with Tesla and xAI in the massive chipmaking endeavour Musk has dubbed Terafab. Analysts say SpaceX needs a massive cash infusion to keep up with the sheer cost of compute, infrastructure and energy needed for its expansion.

stories.headline: CDC pauses dozens of types of lab testing amid agency downsizing
stories.body: The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention has paused diagnostic testing for rabies, monkeypox and more than two dozen other infectious diseases, raising alarm among public health experts about the impact of the agency's dramatic downsizing over the past year. CDC staffing fell by 20 to 25 percent through layoffs, retirements, resignations and non-renewed appointments, with the poxvirus and rabies labs losing about half their prior staff and the malaria branch gutted even further. A government spokesman called the pause temporary and attributed it to a routine review, but the Association of Public Health Laboratories said the agency is pausing more types of tests than ever before and the reasons are not entirely clear. While some paused tests cover common infections for which commercial alternatives exist, the list also includes testing for more exotic agents like parasitic worms. Specialised state labs in New York and California have some capacity to fill the gap, but experts warned the situation would be concerning if it becomes permanent.

stories.headline: Student shoots teacher at Texas high school before fatally shooting himself
stories.body: A 15-year-old student shot a teacher at Hill Country College Preparatory High School in Bulverde, near San Antonio, before turning the gun on himself in an early morning attack on Monday. No other injuries were reported. The teacher was taken to a nearby hospital, though her condition was not immediately disclosed. Comal County Sheriff Mark Reynolds said investigators were working to understand the relationship between the student and the teacher and how the firearm was obtained. Parents gathered at nearby Bulverde Middle School to be reunited with their children after the school was placed on lockdown.

stories.headline: DNA testing confirms serial killer Ted Bundy murdered a Utah teenager in 1974
stories.body: New DNA testing has confirmed that serial killer Ted Bundy was responsible for the 1974 murder of a Utah teenager, resolving a cold case that had remained officially unsolved for more than half a century. The identification adds to the documented toll of Bundy's multi-state killing spree during the 1970s, in which he confessed to murdering at least 30 women and girls across several states before his execution in Florida in 1989. Investigators had long suspected Bundy in the case but lacked the forensic evidence to make a definitive link until advances in DNA technology allowed them to conclusively match samples to the notorious serial killer.

stories.headline: Escribano resigns as Indra president; SEPI proposes former CriteriaCaixa CEO Angel Simon as successor
stories.body: Angel Escribano formally resigned as president and board member of Spain's flagship defence company Indra on Wednesday evening, ending weeks of mounting pressure from the government. The state holding company SEPI immediately proposed Angel Simon, the former CEO of CriteriaCaixa and longtime head of the Agbar water group, to replace him. The emergency board meeting, called during Semana Santa with several directors forced to participate remotely, followed weeks of conflict after SEPI blocked a proposed acquisition of the Escribano family's engineering firm over a conflict of interest. In his farewell letter, Escribano said the events of recent weeks threatened to compromise Indra's objectives and that he was prioritising the company's interest above any personal consideration. The change comes at a critical moment for Spain's defence industry, with billions in contracts at stake and Indra designated as the country's lead defence contractor.

stories.headline: Podemos members overwhelmingly back joining the Por Andalucia coalition ahead of May 17 elections
stories.body: Eighty-one percent of Podemos Andalucia members voted in favour of joining the Por Andalucia coalition alongside Izquierda Unida and Sumar for the Andalusian regional elections on May 17, in a snap consultation that drew nearly 6,000 participants in just 24 hours. The coalition, led by Antonio Maillo, has called a meeting on Thursday to formalise the registration with the electoral board ahead of Friday's deadline. The vote is intended to avoid splitting the left-of-PSOE vote into three separate candidacies, as happened to devastating effect in previous elections. The coalition's stated objective is to unseat PP president Juanma Moreno Bonilla from the Junta de Andalucia.

stories.headline: Spanish intelligence service fired Moroccan-origin agent over fears he could be recruited by Rabat
stories.body: Spain's National Intelligence Centre dismissed an agent of Moroccan origin in the summer of 2023 after an internal investigation concluded his loyalty to Spain and the agency was at minimum uncertain, according to a recently published ruling by the Audiencia Nacional that upheld the decision. The internal report cited the agent's behaviour, his dissatisfaction, the fact that most of his family remained in Morocco, and concerns that these factors could be detected and exploited by Moroccan intelligence services. The dismissal occurred two years after the CNI had warned the government about efforts by Morocco's military intelligence directorate to recruit collaborators in Spain during the diplomatic crisis over the Polisario leader Brahim Gali. In the past 13 years, the CNI has blocked at least six Moroccan citizens from acquiring Spanish nationality after detecting alleged ties to Rabat's espionage services.

stories.headline: Listeria alert on smoked salmon expands to multiple brands across Spain
stories.body: Spain's food safety agency AESAN has expanded a listeria contamination alert on smoked Norwegian salmon initially limited to the Skandia brand to include products sold under the Gourmet, Carrefour and Tora labels. All affected products share the same batch number 361214 with expiry dates in early April 2026 and were manufactured under the same conditions. The bacterium Listeria monocytogenes can cause serious illness, particularly in pregnant women, the elderly and those with weakened immune systems. AESAN has urged consumers who have the affected products at home not to consume them and to seek medical attention if they experience symptoms such as vomiting, diarrhoea or fever. Authorities in both Andalusia and Murcia have issued alerts, and additional Tora brand lots produced under similar conditions are considered suspect.

stories.headline: One dead and three injured after explosion and fire at an Almeria cafeteria
stories.body: One person died and at least three others were injured, one critically, after two explosions and a subsequent fire tore through a cafeteria on Calle Alfareiras in Almeria on Wednesday afternoon. Emergency services including firefighters, bomb disposal specialists, local police and medical teams rushed to the scene after the 112 emergency centre received reports at 5.20pm. Authorities cordoned off a wide area around the premises while investigators worked to determine the cause, with initial hypotheses pointing to a gas cylinder as the likely origin of the blast. The explosions occurred near the Plaza de Toros in the Andalusian capital during what should have been a quiet Semana Santa evening.

stories.headline: Renfe shelves plans for Barcelona-Paris high-speed rail service indefinitely
stories.body: Renfe has abandoned its plans to launch a high-speed train service between Barcelona and Paris, citing accumulated difficulties in the process of homologating its trains in France and the impossibility of establishing a reliable timeline. The Spanish rail operator has released its reserved time slots on the Paris-Lyon corridor and paused the French certification process for its new trains, though it insists the retreat is not permanent. The decision follows last year's suspension of the Barcelona-Perpignan-Toulouse route, which Renfe had previously operated jointly with SNCF until the pandemic. Transport Minister Oscar Puente had already flagged the lack of profitability of the French market for Renfe and the bureaucratic obstacles the company was encountering. The move leaves SNCF as the sole operator of high-speed services between Spain and France.

stories.headline: Union conflict with Renfe blocks restoration of Rodalies commuter rail services in Catalunya
stories.body: A labour dispute between the train drivers' union Semaf and Renfe is preventing the restoration of commuter rail services across several lines in Catalunya, despite infrastructure work being completed weeks ago. Semaf is refusing to approve the service schedules required to put trains back on restored track, initially over safety concerns but now over demands for more driver positions among the 1,200 new contracts Renfe negotiated to end a strike. The conflict has left the R4 line between Martorell and Sant Sadurni d'Anoia still dependent on replacement buses even though the double track at Gelida has been operational since March 14. It also affects the northern end of the R1 line beyond Blanes and has caused ongoing chaos on the coastal stretch between Garraf and Sitges, where trains are running on outdated schedules that cause pileups at single-track sections, forcing delays and mid-route cancellations that strand commuters. User platforms are demanding urgent solutions.

stories.headline: Juve and Camps leaves DO Cava to join Corpinnat, the largest producer to make the switch
stories.body: Juve and Camps, one of the most recognised names in Catalan sparkling wine, has announced it is leaving the Denominacion de Origen Cava to join Corpinnat, the collective brand of select Penedes wineries committed to organic viticulture, long ageing and territorial identity. The move is one of the most significant in the Catalan wine sector in recent years, making Juve and Camps the largest producer by volume to join Corpinnat, which now counts 22 member wineries. CEO Meritxell Juve said the change was a natural evolution of a century-old trajectory focused on quality and terroir. Corpinnat operates under one of the world's most demanding regulatory frameworks, requiring fully organic and hand-harvested grapes, extended ageing periods, and annual external audits. The DO Cava acknowledged the historic contribution of Juve and Camps while defending the solidity of its own model.

stories.headline: Barcelona plans its first ultra-fast public EV charging station on the Ronda de Dalt
stories.body: Barcelona has announced plans for its first public ultra-fast electric vehicle charging station on the Ronda de Dalt ring road, capable of serving up to 300 vehicles per day with charges completed in approximately 15 minutes. The project forms part of the city's push to accelerate the transition to electric mobility, providing high-speed charging infrastructure that has until now been available only at private or semi-private locations. The station will be a significant addition to Barcelona's charging network as the city prepares for increasingly strict low-emission zone regulations.
